title: Externalities & information failure
summary:
## The big idea A factory sells cement to a builder; both are happy. The village downwind breathing the dust was never asked. Costs (or benefits) that land on **third parties** outside a transaction are **externalities**, and because buyers and sellers ignore them, the market settles at the *wrong quantity*. Add **information failure** — people not knowing what's good for them, or one side of a deal knowing more than the other — and you have the two most heavily examined market failures at A2. ## The four-curve toolkit Everything runs on the gap between *private* and *social*: - **MPC** — marginal private cost (what the producer pays). **MSC** — marginal social cost = MPC **+ external cost**. - **MPB** — marginal private benefit (what the buyer gains). **MSB** — marginal social benefit = MPB **+ external benefit**. The market settles where **MPB = MPC**. Society's best point — the **social optimum** — is where **MSB = MSC**. When any external cost or benefit exists, those two points differ, and the gap creates a **deadweight welfare loss**. ## Negative externality of production (pollution) The factory pays for labour and materials (MPC) but not for the river it poisons — so **MSC lies above MPC**. Left alone, the market **over-produces**:
**What each part means:** the market ignores the external cost and settles where **MPB = MPC**, at quantity **Qmkt** (grey dot). But the true cost curve is the dashed **MSC** — MPC *plus* the pollution damage (the vertical gap between the yellow curves). Society's optimum is where **MSB = MSC**: the smaller quantity **Qopt** (orange dot). For every unit between Qopt and Qmkt, social cost exceeds the benefit — the **shaded red triangle** is the **deadweight welfare loss** from over-production. Note the triangle always **points at the social optimum**.
## The other three cases — same logic, different curve - **Negative externality of consumption** (smoking, loud music): harm falls on bystanders, so **MSB lies below MPB** → the market **over-consumes**. This is the **demerit good** diagram. - **Positive externality of production** (a firm trains workers who later benefit other employers; bees pollinating orchards): **MSC lies below MPC** → **under-production**. - **Positive externality of consumption** (education, vaccination): benefits spill over to everyone, so **MSB lies above MPB** → the market **under-consumes**. This is the **merit good** diagram. Exam drill for *any* of the four: label all curves, mark **both** quantities (market and optimum), shade the **welfare-loss triangle** pointing at the optimum. **Correcting them:** negative → **Pigouvian tax** (shift MPC up to MSC), **tradable pollution permits**, regulation, bans; positive → **subsidy** (close the MPB/MSB gap), **direct provision**, information campaigns. Every fix inherits the AS Unit 3 caveats: measuring the externality is guesswork (the **information problem**), enforcement costs money, and government failure lurks. (Unit 8 dives deeper.) ## Information failure Markets clear at the *right* quantity only if buyers and sellers know what they're doing. **Information failure** means decisions are based on missing or wrong information — the deep reason merit goods are under-consumed (people underestimate the long-run benefit of education) and demerit goods over-consumed (smokers underestimate the harm). ### Asymmetric information — one side knows more - **Adverse selection** — the *hidden information* problem, arising **before** a deal. Akerlof's 'market for **lemons**': used-car sellers know which cars are bad; buyers can't tell, so they offer only an average price; owners of good cars withdraw; the market fills with lemons and may collapse. Same logic in insurance: the sickest are keenest to buy, pushing premiums up until the healthy drop out. - **Moral hazard** — the *hidden action* problem, arising **after** a deal. Once insured, people take more risks; once banks expect bail-outs, they lend recklessly (2008's expensive lesson). The party who doesn't bear the full consequences changes behaviour. **Market and policy responses:** **signalling** (warranties, brands, qualifications — the informed side proves quality), **screening** (insurers' medical checks, excess clauses), compulsory **disclosure** rules, and regulation. None works perfectly — which is exactly the evaluation point. ## Why it matters The MSB/MSC diagrams are arguably the most-drawn diagrams in Paper 3 and 4, and adverse selection vs moral hazard is a favourite discriminator between average and strong candidates. Master the four cases until you can draw any of them, correctly shaded, in under a minute.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **MSC = MPC + external cost; MSB = MPB + external benefit.** Market: MPB = MPC. Optimum: **MSB = MSC**. - Negative **production** (pollution): MSC > MPC → over-production. Negative **consumption** (demerit): MSB < MPB → over-consumption. - Positive **production** (training): MSC < MPC → under-production. Positive **consumption** (merit): MSB > MPB → under-consumption. - Always: both quantities + shaded **welfare-loss triangle pointing at the optimum**. - Fixes: tax/permits/regulation (negative); subsidy/provision/information (positive) — all limited by the **information problem**. - **Adverse selection** = hidden *information*, before the deal (lemons, insurance). **Moral hazard** = hidden *action*, after (insured risk-taking, bank bail-outs). - Responses: signalling, screening, disclosure, regulation.

keyTerms.definition: A cost or benefit imposed on a third party not part of the transaction.
Marginal private cost plus any external cost of production.
Marginal private benefit plus any external benefit of consumption.
The allocatively efficient output where MSB = MSC.
The loss of social welfare when output differs from the social optimum.
When agents lack full or accurate information, leading to poor decisions.
When one party in a transaction has more information than the other.
A pre-contract problem where hidden information leads the wrong types to trade (e.g. lemons).
A post-contract problem where a party takes more risk because they don't bear the full consequences.

diagrams: **Negative externality of production:** MSC above MPC; market output where MPB=MPC exceeds the optimum where MSB=MSC; shade the welfare-loss triangle.
**Positive externality of consumption (merit good):** MSB above MPB; market under-consumes; shade the welfare loss and show the subsidy/provision needed.
**Corrective tax/subsidy:** show a Pigouvian tax shifting MPC up to MSC (or a subsidy shifting MPB up to MSB) to reach the social optimum.
tips: Draw externality diagrams **fully labelled** (MPC, MSC, MPB, MSB), mark **both quantities**, and **shade the welfare-loss triangle** pointing toward the social optimum.
Classify carefully: is it an externality of **production or consumption**, positive or negative? That fixes which curve diverges.
Distinguish **adverse selection (hidden information, before)** from **moral hazard (hidden action, after)** with an example each.
Link merit/demerit goods to **information failure + externalities** for a complete answer.
Evaluate corrective policies with the **information problem** (setting the right tax/subsidy) and **government failure**.

revisionNotes:
# Externalities & information failure — quick revision

**The toolkit**
- MSC = MPC + external cost. MSB = MPB + external benefit.
- Market output: MPB = MPC. **Social optimum: MSB = MSC.** Gap → deadweight welfare loss.

**The four cases (learn which curve splits)**
| Case | Curves | Market outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Negative production (pollution) | MSC above MPC | over-production |
| Negative consumption (demerit: smoking) | MSB below MPB | over-consumption |
| Positive production (training, bees) | MSC below MPC | under-production |
| Positive consumption (merit: education, vaccines) | MSB above MPB | under-consumption |

- Diagram drill: label curves, mark Qmkt *and* Qopt, shade the triangle **pointing at Qopt**.

**Fixes**
- Negative: **Pigouvian tax** (MPC→MSC), tradable permits, regulation/bans.
- Positive: **subsidy** (close the gap), direct provision, information.
- Caveats: externality unmeasurable (information problem), enforcement cost, government failure.

**Information failure**
- Merit/demerit goods = consumers misjudge long-run benefits/harms.
- **Adverse selection** — hidden information, *before* contract: lemons market, sick buying insurance.
- **Moral hazard** — hidden action, *after* contract: insured take risks, banks expecting bail-outs.
- Responses: **signalling** (warranties, qualifications), **screening** (medical checks, excess), disclosure laws, regulation.
questions:
# Practice questions — Externalities & information failure **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** A negative externality of production exists when: - A — marginal private cost exceeds marginal social cost - B — marginal social cost exceeds marginal private cost - C — marginal social benefit exceeds marginal private benefit - D — a firm makes abnormal profit
▸ Show answer

B. External costs (pollution) are added on top of the producer's private costs, so MSC > MPC and the market over-produces. C describes a positive consumption externality.

**2.** Vaccination is under-consumed in a free market principally because: - A — its marginal social benefit exceeds its marginal private benefit - B — its marginal private cost is zero - C — it is a public good - D — suppliers have monopoly power
▸ Show answer

A. Each vaccination protects others too — an external benefit individuals ignore when deciding, so MSB > MPB and consumption stops short of the social optimum. (It is excludable and rival, so not a public good.)

**3.** After buying comprehensive car insurance, a driver starts parking carelessly. This behaviour is an example of: - A — adverse selection - B — moral hazard - C — the free-rider problem - D — loss aversion
▸ Show answer

B. A hidden action after the contract: the insured driver no longer bears the full cost of carelessness. Adverse selection would be risky drivers being the keenest buyers before the contract.

**4.** The socially optimal level of output occurs where: - A — MPB = MPC - B — MSB = MSC - C — MSB = MPC - D — price is minimised
▸ Show answer

B. The optimum weighs all benefits against all costs, including external ones. MPB = MPC (A) is where the unregulated market settles.

## Paper 3 / 4 style — data response > A tannery on a river discharges untreated waste, killing fish stocks that 2,000 downstream families depend on. An environmental agency estimates the damage at \$4 per hide processed. The government is weighing three options: a \$4 per hide tax; a legal limit on discharge; or a scheme of tradable discharge permits. The tannery employs 800 workers and exports most of its output. **(a)** Define 'negative externality of production'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain, with the aid of a diagram, why the tannery produces more than the socially optimal output. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse how the \$4 per hide tax would move the market toward the social optimum. **[6]** **(d)** Assess which of the three policy options the government should adopt. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) A cost imposed on third parties (downstream families) by production, ignored by producer and buyer. [1+1]

(b) MSC = MPC + \$4 damage → MSC above MPC; market output at MPB = MPC (Qmkt) exceeds optimum at MSB = MSC (Qopt); shade welfare loss. [diagram 2, explanation 2]

(c) Tax = estimated external cost → shifts MPC up to MSC ('internalising' the externality); price rises, output falls to Qopt; welfare loss eliminated; revenue can compensate/clean up. Note reliance on the \$4 estimate being right. [6]

(d) Tax: efficient, revenue, but needs accurate valuation and may just relocate pollution. Regulation: certain limit, but no incentive to cut below it; inspection costs. Permits: cap certainty + least-cost abatement via trading (EU ETS logic), but the cap must be set right and policed; thin market with one big firm weakens trading. Judgement weighing employment/export context; any justified choice scores. [8]

## Paper 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain, with diagrams, the difference between a negative externality of consumption and a positive externality of consumption. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether indirect taxation is the best way to correct the market failure caused by demerit goods. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Demerit: MSB below MPB → over-consumption (smoking). Merit: MSB above MPB → under-consumption (education). Both diagrams with quantities and welfare loss.

(b) For: raises price toward the social optimum, generates revenue, market-based. Against: inelastic/addictive demand blunts it; regressive; black markets; right rate unknowable. Alternatives: regulation, information campaigns, nudges — often a package works best. Reasoned judgement.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain, with examples, the difference between adverse selection and moral hazard. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether government intervention can successfully correct the problems caused by asymmetric information. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Hidden information before the contract (lemons; sick insurance buyers) vs hidden action after (risk-taking once insured; banks expecting bail-outs). Clear timeline distinction + examples.

(b) Tools: compulsory disclosure, quality standards/licensing, deposit insurance with regulation, compulsory insurance pools. Limits: government also lacks information; regulation invites capture and box-ticking; markets partly self-correct via signalling/screening (warranties, brands). Judgement: intervention helps where stakes are high (finance, health) but rarely eliminates the problem.
